questions.prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
The Mussel Survey
When biologist Dana Price stood knee-deep in Clear Fork Creek, she was not looking for fish. She was looking for freshwater mussels, animals that spend most of their lives partly buried in the streambed. Many people do not notice mussels because they do not move quickly or make noise. Yet they can reveal important information about the health of a stream.
Freshwater mussels filter water as they feed. They can be harmed by pollution, heavy sediment, low oxygen, and sudden changes in water flow. Because many species live for years in the same area, their presence or absence can tell scientists more than a single water sample can. A water sample shows conditions at one moment; mussels can reflect conditions over time.
Price and her team surveyed five sections of the creek. In shaded areas with gravel beds and steady flow, they found several mussel species and young mussels, which suggested that reproduction was occurring. In a section downstream from a construction site, they found fewer mussels and more fine sediment covering the streambed. The team did not blame the construction site automatically, but the pattern showed where more monitoring was needed.
The survey helped local officials understand that stream health is not measured by one number. It depends on water quality, habitat, flow, and living organisms. The quiet mussels in Clear Fork Creek became evidence that the stream’s condition could be studied over time, not just tested once and forgotten.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Freshwater mussels can help scientists understand stream health because they reflect conditions over time.
choices.B: Freshwater mussels are useful only because they move quickly through streams.
choices.C: Clear Fork Creek was tested once and then declared perfectly healthy.
choices.D: Construction sites always destroy every mussel species in nearby creeks.

questions.correctAnswerText: Freshwater mussels can help scientists understand stream health because they reflect conditions over time.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that mussels live in one area for years and can show long-term stream conditions.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Mussel Survey.”
Why can mussels reveal information that a single water sample may not show?

choices.A: Mussels live in one area for years and can reflect long-term conditions.
choices.B: Mussels record human speech near the creek.
choices.C: Mussels change the color of the water every hour.
choices.D: Mussels appear only when scientists add them to a stream.

questions.correctAnswerText: Mussels live in one area for years and can reflect long-term conditions.
questions.explanation: A single water sample shows one moment, while mussels can reflect conditions over time.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The Mussel Survey.”
Which TWO conditions can harm freshwater mussels?

choices.A: Heavy sediment
choices.B: Low oxygen
choices.C: Shaded streambanks
choices.D: Gravel beds

questions.correctAnswerText: Heavy sediment | Low oxygen
questions.explanation: The passage lists pollution, heavy sediment, low oxygen, and sudden flow changes as conditions that can harm mussels.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Mussel Survey.”
What can be inferred from the discovery of young mussels in some parts of the creek?

choices.A: Mussels were successfully reproducing in those areas.
choices.B: The creek had no living organisms before the survey.
choices.C: Mussels had been placed there by construction workers.
choices.D: The shaded sections had become unsafe for all stream life.

questions.correctAnswerText: Mussels were successfully reproducing in those areas.
questions.explanation: The passage says young mussels suggested that reproduction was occurring.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Mussel Survey.”
In the sentence “their presence or absence can tell scientists more than a single water sample can,” presence most nearly means [D1].
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=being there
questions.explanation: In context, presence means that mussels are found in the area.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Mussel Survey.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: The team found several mussel species and young mussels in shaded gravel areas.
tiles.B: Fewer mussels were found downstream from a construction site.
tiles.C: Price did not automatically blame the construction site.
tiles.D: The team removed all water from the creek.

targets.T1: Evidence of healthier habitat in some sections
targets.T2: Evidence of an area needing more monitoring
targets.T3: Evidence that the team avoided an unsupported conclusion

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=The team found several mussel species and young mussels in shaded gravel areas.; T2=Fewer mussels were found downstream from a construction site.; T3=Price did not automatically blame the construction site.
questions.explanation: The tiles match evidence of healthier habitat, a section needing monitoring, and careful reasoning.

questions.prompt:
Read the workplace document and answer the question.
Production Hold Procedure
To: Packaging Line Staff, Quality Technicians, and Shift Supervisors
From: Plant Quality Manager
Subject: Production Hold Procedure
Beginning May 14, any product batch with a possible labeling, sealing, weight, or ingredient problem must be placed on production hold before it leaves the packaging area. A production hold means the batch cannot be shipped, moved to finished inventory, or released to customers until quality staff review it.
Employees should start a hold when they notice a missing label, damaged seal, incorrect date code, unusual package weight, or ingredient lot number that does not match the batch record. The employee must attach a red hold tag to the pallet and notify the shift supervisor immediately.
The hold tag must include the batch number, product name, time the issue was found, employee initials, and a short description of the concern. Do not write guesses about who caused the problem. Describe only what was observed. For example, write “date code missing on 12 packages,” not “printer operator forgot to check labels.”
Only a quality technician or plant quality manager may remove a hold tag. If the batch passes review, it may be released. If the concern is confirmed, the batch will be corrected, reworked, or discarded according to company policy.
This procedure protects customers, employees, and the company. It also helps the plant identify recurring problems before they become larger failures.
What is the main purpose of the document?

choices.A: To explain when and how staff should place product batches on hold for quality review
choices.B: To allow employees to ship batches with missing labels immediately
choices.C: To require workers to guess who caused every production problem
choices.D: To tell quality technicians to stop reviewing product batches

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain when and how staff should place product batches on hold for quality review
questions.explanation: The document explains the production hold process for possible quality problems.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Production Hold Procedure.”
Which situation should start a production hold?

choices.A: A missing label on packages in a batch
choices.B: A normal lunch break for packaging staff
choices.C: A shipment that has already passed review
choices.D: A batch that has no observed concern

questions.correctAnswerText: A missing label on packages in a batch
questions.explanation: The procedure lists missing labels as a reason to start a production hold.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Production Hold Procedure.”
Which TWO details must be included on the hold tag?

choices.A: The batch number
choices.B: A short description of the concern
choices.C: A guess about who caused the problem
choices.D: The customer’s home address

questions.correctAnswerText: The batch number | A short description of the concern
questions.explanation: The hold tag must include the batch number and a short description, along with other details.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Production Hold Procedure.”
Who may remove a hold tag?

choices.A: Only a quality technician or plant quality manager
choices.B: Any customer waiting for the shipment
choices.C: Any employee who wants the pallet moved
choices.D: Only the delivery driver

questions.correctAnswerText: Only a quality technician or plant quality manager
questions.explanation: The document says only these roles may remove a hold tag.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Production Hold Procedure.”
Which sentence best explains the reason for the procedure?

choices.A: “Beginning May 14, any product batch with a possible labeling, sealing, weight, or ingredient problem must be placed on production hold.”
choices.B: “The employee must attach a red hold tag to the pallet and notify the shift supervisor immediately.”
choices.C: “This procedure protects customers, employees, and the company.”
choices.D: “Do not write guesses about who caused the problem.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “This procedure protects customers, employees, and the company.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly explains the purpose of the hold procedure.

questions.prompt:
Read the argument and answer the question.
Offer Paid Job Shadow Days for Career Changers
Employers should offer paid job shadow days for adults considering hard-to-fill careers. A job shadow day allows a potential applicant to observe a workplace, ask questions, and understand daily responsibilities before committing to training or applying. This approach can help people make informed decisions about careers such as manufacturing, medical billing, building maintenance, logistics, or early childhood education.
Many adults hesitate to enter a new field because they do not know what the work is really like. A job title alone may not explain the pace, tools, customer contact, schedule, or physical demands of the role. A paid shadow day can remove mystery while respecting the visitor’s time. People who are unemployed, underemployed, or supporting families may not be able to spend a full day observing without compensation.
Employers also benefit. A person who understands the work before applying is less likely to quit after the first week because the job was not what they expected. Job shadowing can improve the match between applicant and employer, which may reduce turnover.
Some managers may worry that visitors slow down workers or create liability concerns. Those concerns can be addressed with short visits, signed agreements, safety rules, and carefully chosen observation tasks. A paid job shadow day is not a full training program. It is a practical bridge between curiosity and commitment.
What is the author’s main claim?

choices.A: Employers should offer paid job shadow days to help career changers make informed decisions and improve job matching.
choices.B: Employers should hire all visitors without applications.
choices.C: Job shadow days should replace all training programs.
choices.D: Adults should choose careers based only on job titles.

questions.correctAnswerText: Employers should offer paid job shadow days to help career changers make informed decisions and improve job matching.
questions.explanation: The author argues that paid shadow days help people understand jobs and help employers find better matches.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Offer Paid Job Shadow Days for Career Changers.”
Which evidence best supports the claim that job titles may not give enough information?

choices.A: A job title may not explain the pace, tools, customer contact, schedule, or physical demands of a role.
choices.B: A paid shadow day is not a full training program.
choices.C: Some managers worry that visitors may slow down workers.
choices.D: Employers can use signed agreements.

questions.correctAnswerText: A job title may not explain the pace, tools, customer contact, schedule, or physical demands of a role.
questions.explanation: This evidence supports the claim that observation gives information a title alone may not provide.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Offer Paid Job Shadow Days for Career Changers.”
Which TWO safeguards does the author suggest?

choices.A: Short visits
choices.B: Signed agreements
choices.C: Allowing visitors to perform unsupervised work
choices.D: Removing safety rules during observation

questions.correctAnswerText: Short visits | Signed agreements
questions.explanation: The author suggests short visits, signed agreements, safety rules, and chosen observation tasks.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Offer Paid Job Shadow Days for Career Changers.”
How does the author respond to concerns about visitors slowing workers or creating liability problems?

choices.A: By suggesting short visits, agreements, safety rules, and carefully chosen observation tasks
choices.B: By saying workplaces should ignore safety concerns
choices.C: By arguing that visitors should replace trained employees
choices.D: By recommending that visitors observe dangerous tasks alone

questions.correctAnswerText: By suggesting short visits, agreements, safety rules, and carefully chosen observation tasks
questions.explanation: These safeguards address concerns about disruption and liability.

questions.prompt:

The observation room has a clock above the door and two chairs near the wall.

questions.correct: D1=A regional logistics company found that applicants who completed a paid shadow day were more likely to remain employed after three months than applicants who had only read the job posting.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=A regional logistics company found that applicants who completed a paid shadow day were more likely to remain employed after three months than applicants who had only read the job posting.
questions.explanation: This evidence strengthens the claim that shadow days can improve job matching and retention.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Offer Paid Job Shadow Days for Career Changers.”
Which assumption is most important to the author’s argument?

choices.A: People make better career decisions when they understand what a job actually involves.
choices.B: Every visitor will immediately become an expert after one day.
choices.C: Employers should stop using interviews and applications.
choices.D: Job descriptions always provide complete information about workplace reality.

questions.correctAnswerText: People make better career decisions when they understand what a job actually involves.
questions.explanation: The argument depends on the idea that direct observation improves career decision-making.

questions.prompt:
Read the literary excerpt and answer the question.
The Quiet Cue
Eli had worked backstage at the small theater for most of the season, taping cables, sweeping sawdust, and carrying props that looked heavy but were mostly hollow. He liked backstage work because the audience never saw him. If something went wrong, he could fix it in the shadows.
On the final rehearsal for the winter play, the stage manager handed him a headset. “You’ll call the snow cue tonight,” she said. “When the second bell rings, say, ‘Snow ready.’ When Lena raises her lantern, say, ‘Snow go.’”
Eli looked up at the paper snow hanging in bags above the stage. One wrong cue could drop it too early, too late, or not at all. “I thought Marco was doing that.”
“Marco is home sick,” she said. “You know the scene.”
During rehearsal, Eli stood beside the curtain with the headset pressing one ear. The first bell rang. Actors crossed the stage. The second bell sounded from the prop tower. Eli’s mouth went dry. “Snow ready,” he whispered.
“Louder,” the stage manager said through the headset.
He repeated it. Lena raised the lantern. Eli watched the light catch her face, then said, “Snow go.”
Above the stage, the bags opened. Paper flakes fell slowly through the blue light. No one in the audience would know who had called the cue. That was the point, Eli realized. Some responsibilities were successful because they disappeared into the scene.
What is the main conflict in the excerpt?

choices.A: Eli must take responsibility for an unseen but important backstage cue.
choices.B: Eli tries to act in the winter play without rehearsing.
choices.C: Eli argues with Lena about the lantern prop.
choices.D: Eli refuses to work backstage because the audience cannot see him.

questions.correctAnswerText: Eli must take responsibility for an unseen but important backstage cue.
questions.explanation: The excerpt centers on Eli’s nervousness and success in calling the snow cue.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Quiet Cue.”
Why does Eli prefer backstage work at the beginning of the excerpt?

choices.A: He likes being able to fix problems without being seen by the audience.
choices.B: He wants to replace every actor in the play.
choices.C: He dislikes every part of theater work.
choices.D: He believes props should never be used.

questions.correctAnswerText: He likes being able to fix problems without being seen by the audience.
questions.explanation: Eli prefers working in the shadows where the audience does not see him.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Quiet Cue.”
What does the stage manager’s instruction “Louder” show?

choices.A: Eli needs to communicate clearly even though he is nervous.
choices.B: Eli has already ruined the entire scene.
choices.C: The actors cannot hear the bell.
choices.D: The snow cue has been canceled.

questions.correctAnswerText: Eli needs to communicate clearly even though he is nervous.
questions.explanation: The stage manager’s instruction shows that whispering is not enough for the cue to work.

questions.prompt:

Refer to “The Quiet Cue.”

choices.A: He notices that one wrong cue could drop the snow too early or too late.
choices.B: His mouth goes dry before he speaks.
choices.C: He sweeps sawdust earlier in the season.
choices.D: Paper flakes fall slowly through the blue light.
choices.E: The props look heavy but are mostly hollow.

questions.correctAnswerText: He notices that one wrong cue could drop the snow too early or too late. | His mouth goes dry before he speaks.
questions.explanation: Both details reveal Eli’s anxiety about the responsibility.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Quiet Cue.”
What does Eli realize at the end of the excerpt?

choices.A: Some important work is successful precisely because it supports the scene without drawing attention.
choices.B: Backstage work matters only when the audience knows who did it.
choices.C: The snow cue should have happened before the second bell.
choices.D: The stage manager should never use headsets.

questions.correctAnswerText: Some important work is successful precisely because it supports the scene without drawing attention.
questions.explanation: Eli realizes that invisible backstage work can be essential.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Quiet Cue.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Eli’s mouth goes dry before he says the cue.
tiles.B: The stage manager tells Eli to say “Snow go” when Lena raises her lantern.
tiles.C: Paper flakes fall slowly through the blue light.
tiles.D: Eli walks onto the stage and stops the play.

targets.T2: Evidence of the cue’s timing rule
targets.T3: Evidence that the cue works as intended

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Eli’s mouth goes dry before he says the cue.; T2=The stage manager tells Eli to say “Snow go” when Lena raises her lantern.; T3=Paper flakes fall slowly through the blue light.
questions.explanation: The tiles show anxiety, timing guidance, and the successful cue.

questions.prompt:
Read the informational passage and answer the question.
Understanding a Home Inspection Report
A home inspection report is a written summary of a house’s visible condition at the time of inspection. Buyers often receive one before deciding whether to continue with a purchase, request repairs, or ask for more information. The report may cover the roof, foundation, plumbing, electrical system, heating and cooling equipment, windows, doors, drainage, and safety concerns.
A report does not usually tell a buyer whether to buy the house. Instead, it identifies observed conditions. A sentence such as “water staining noted below upstairs bathroom” does not automatically prove that a current leak exists, but it does show that the area deserves attention. The buyer may need to ask for repair records, request a specialist evaluation, or discuss options with an agent.
It is also important to notice the difference between major concerns and routine maintenance. A missing outlet cover is not the same as outdated wiring. A loose handrail is not the same as foundation movement. Good inspection reports often include photos, descriptions, and recommendations so that buyers can understand the level of concern.
The report has limits. Inspectors generally cannot see behind finished walls or predict every future problem. Still, reading the report carefully can help buyers ask better questions and avoid being surprised by issues that were visible before purchase.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: A home inspection report helps buyers understand visible conditions, possible concerns, and needed follow-up.
choices.B: A home inspection report guarantees that a house will never have problems.
choices.C: Buyers should ignore inspection reports and rely only on the asking price.
choices.D: Inspectors can see every hidden problem behind finished walls.

questions.correctAnswerText: A home inspection report helps buyers understand visible conditions, possible concerns, and needed follow-up.
questions.explanation: The passage explains what reports identify, how buyers should interpret findings, and what follow-up may be needed.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Home Inspection Report.”
What does the passage say a home inspection report usually does?

choices.A: Identifies observed conditions
choices.B: Guarantees that the buyer should purchase the house
choices.C: Sets the final sale price automatically
choices.D: Replaces all specialist evaluations

questions.correctAnswerText: Identifies observed conditions
questions.explanation: The passage says the report identifies observed conditions rather than deciding whether the buyer should buy.

choices.A: To show the difference between routine maintenance and a more serious concern
choices.B: To argue that all electrical issues are equally serious
choices.C: To prove that outlet covers are never mentioned in reports
choices.D: To show that buyers should ignore safety concerns

questions.correctAnswerText: To show the difference between routine maintenance and a more serious concern
questions.explanation: The comparison helps readers distinguish minor issues from major concerns.

questions.prompt:

choices.A: “The report may cover the roof, foundation, plumbing, electrical system, heating and cooling equipment, windows, doors, drainage, and safety concerns.”
choices.B: “The buyer may need to ask for repair records, request a specialist evaluation, or discuss options with an agent.”
choices.C: “Inspectors generally cannot see behind finished walls or predict every future problem.”
choices.D: “Good inspection reports often include photos, descriptions, and recommendations.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Inspectors generally cannot see behind finished walls or predict every future problem.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly explains the limits of an inspection report.

choices.B: It tells a story about one family moving into a new house.
choices.C: It lists real estate agents in order of experience.
choices.D: It argues that buyers should never request repairs.

questions.explanation: The passage defines reports, explains examples, and discusses limitations.

questions.prompt:
Read the editing passage and choose the best words for the blanks.
Conference Room Setup Request
(1) Employees who need a conference room setup should submit a request at least two business days before the meeting. (2) The request should include the meeting date, start time, expected number of attendees, and seating arrangement. (3) Facilities staff use this information to prepare chairs, tables, and presentation equipment. (4) [D1], requests submitted late may not be completed before the meeting begins. (5) The confirmation email [D2] sent after facilities staff review the request. (6) Employees should report setup changes as soon as possible.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=However; D2=will be
questions.explanation: “However” contrasts the recommended deadline with late requests, and “will be sent” is grammatically correct.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Conference Room Setup Request.”
Which revision of sentence 6 is most concise while keeping the meaning?

choices.A: Employees should report setup changes as soon as possible.
choices.B: Employees should make reports of setup changes at the soonest possible time.
choices.C: Setup changes should be reported by employees as soon as possible in timing.
choices.D: Employees, if there are setup changes, should report those setup changes soon.

questions.correctAnswerText: Employees should report setup changes as soon as possible.
questions.explanation: This sentence is concise and preserves the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses correct subject-verb agreement?

choices.A: The chairs near the side wall needs to be moved.
choices.B: The list of room requests are on the clipboard.
choices.C: The employees who reserve rooms receive confirmation emails.
choices.D: The folder of setup diagrams have been updated.

questions.correctAnswerText: The employees who reserve rooms receive confirmation emails.
questions.explanation: The plural subject “employees” correctly takes the plural verb “receive.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the clearest pronoun reference?

choices.A: When Serena called Morgan after the meeting, she changed the setup request.
choices.B: Serena called Morgan after the meeting, and Serena changed the setup request.
choices.C: She changed it when Serena called Morgan after the meeting.
choices.D: After the meeting, she called her and changed it.

questions.correctAnswerText: Serena called Morgan after the meeting, and Serena changed the setup request.
questions.explanation: Repeating “Serena” makes clear who changed the request.

questions.prompt:

The meeting was moved from Room 204 to Room 110. [D1], facilities staff delivered the projector to the new location.

questions.explanation: “Therefore” shows that the projector was delivered to the new location because the meeting moved.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?

choices.A: The room was reserved for noon; however, the meeting began at one.
choices.B: The room was reserved for noon however the meeting began at one.
choices.C: The room was reserved for noon, however the meeting began at one.
choices.D: The room was reserved for noon; however the meeting, began at one.

questions.correctAnswerText: The room was reserved for noon; however, the meeting began at one.
questions.explanation: A semicolon before “however” and a comma after it correctly join the clauses.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is a complete sentence?

choices.A: Because the projector was missing.
choices.B: After the meeting organizer arrived.
choices.C: The assistant placed extra chairs near the door.
choices.D: While checking the seating diagram.

questions.correctAnswerText: The assistant placed extra chairs near the door.
questions.explanation: This is the only complete sentence.

questions.prompt: Which sentence maintains consistent verb tense?

choices.A: The coordinator checked the request and updates the room yesterday.
choices.B: The coordinator checks the request and updated the room yesterday.
choices.C: The coordinator checked the request and updated the room yesterday.
choices.D: The coordinator had checked the request and updates the room yesterday.

questions.correctAnswerText: The coordinator checked the request and updated the room yesterday.
questions.explanation: Both verbs are in past tense and match “yesterday.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses parallel structure?

choices.A: The request asks for meeting date, room layout, and whether equipment is needed.
choices.B: The request asks for meeting date, room layout, and equipment needs.
choices.C: The request asks for dating the meeting, room layout, and to need equipment.
choices.D: The request asks for meeting date, a room that is arranged, and equipment that is needed.

questions.explanation: The list uses parallel noun phrases.

questions.prompt:
Read the paired passages and answer the question.
Passage A: Use Apprenticeship Hiring for Technical Roles
A manufacturing company should create apprenticeship hiring tracks for technical roles that are difficult to fill. Instead of searching only for applicants who already have every skill, the company could hire promising candidates, pay them during structured training, and pair them with experienced mentors. This approach would help build the workforce the company needs.
Apprenticeships can open doors for people who have ability but lack direct experience. A person with strong math skills, careful attention to detail, and a good attendance record may become an excellent machine technician with the right training. Requiring years of experience for every entry-level technical job can screen out capable workers before they have a chance to learn.
The company can protect quality by setting clear milestones. Apprentices should demonstrate safety knowledge, equipment procedures, troubleshooting steps, and documentation skills before advancing. A structured program would not lower standards; it would teach standards. If the company invests in training, it may reduce vacancies and build employee loyalty.
Passage B: Hire Experienced Technicians First
A manufacturing company should focus on hiring experienced technicians rather than building an apprenticeship track. Technical roles affect production quality, safety, and delivery deadlines. A worker who is still learning may need close supervision, and mistakes on complex equipment can be expensive. The company should prioritize applicants who can contribute immediately.
Apprenticeship programs also require time from the most skilled employees. Mentors must explain procedures, check work, answer questions, and document progress. During busy production periods, this may pull experienced technicians away from urgent repairs and preventive maintenance. The company could end up slowing the very workers it depends on most.
There are other ways to address hiring needs. The company can offer referral bonuses, improve pay for experienced technicians, partner with trade schools for candidates who already have basic training, and provide short onboarding for new hires. These steps may fill positions faster than creating a full apprenticeship system. Training has value, but a company facing immediate technical shortages should hire proven workers first.
What is the central difference between the two passages?

choices.A: Passage A supports building apprenticeships for technical roles, while Passage B argues the company should prioritize experienced workers.
choices.B: Passage A argues that technical roles require no standards, while Passage B argues that training should be banned.
choices.C: Passage A supports closing the factory, while Passage B supports eliminating all mentors.
choices.D: Passage A focuses on home inspections, while Passage B focuses on conference room setup.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passage A supports building apprenticeships for technical roles, while Passage B argues the company should prioritize experienced workers.
questions.explanation: Passage A favors developing workers through training; Passage B favors hiring people who can contribute immediately.

questions.prompt:

choices.A: A person with strong math skills and attention to detail may become an excellent technician with the right training.
choices.B: Mistakes on complex equipment can be expensive.
choices.C: Mentors must spend time checking work.
choices.D: Referral bonuses may attract experienced technicians.

questions.correctAnswerText: A person with strong math skills and attention to detail may become an excellent technician with the right training.
questions.explanation: This evidence supports Passage A’s claim that apprenticeships can develop capable workers.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to the paired passages about apprenticeship hiring.
Which TWO claims from Passage B are supported with reasons or evidence?

choices.A: Technical roles affect production quality, safety, and delivery deadlines.
choices.B: Apprenticeship programs may take time away from skilled employees.
choices.C: Apprenticeships guarantee that workers will make no mistakes.
choices.D: Experienced technicians never need onboarding.

questions.correctAnswerText: Technical roles affect production quality, safety, and delivery deadlines. | Apprenticeship programs may take time away from skilled employees.
questions.explanation: Passage B supports both claims as reasons to prioritize experienced hires.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about apprenticeship hiring.
Which statement from Passage A directly addresses the concern that apprenticeships may lower standards?

choices.A: “The company could hire promising candidates, pay them during structured training, and pair them with experienced mentors.”
choices.B: “Apprentices should demonstrate safety knowledge, equipment procedures, troubleshooting steps, and documentation skills before advancing.”
choices.C: “A person with strong math skills, careful attention to detail, and a good attendance record may become an excellent machine technician.”
choices.D: “If the company invests in training, it may reduce vacancies and build employee loyalty.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Apprentices should demonstrate safety knowledge, equipment procedures, troubleshooting steps, and documentation skills before advancing.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly addresses standards by requiring apprentices to demonstrate key skills.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about apprenticeship hiring.
Which statement is a supported inference from both passages?

choices.A: Both authors recognize that technical work requires skill, but they disagree about whether the company should develop or hire that skill.
choices.B: Both authors believe technical roles can be filled without training or experience.
choices.C: Both authors argue that mentors have unlimited free time.
choices.D: Both authors think production quality does not matter.

questions.correctAnswerText: Both authors recognize that technical work requires skill, but they disagree about whether the company should develop or hire that skill.
questions.explanation: Both passages treat technical skill as important but recommend different approaches.

choices.A: Data comparing apprenticeship completion, technician vacancy rates, error rates, mentor time, and experienced-hire retention

questions.prompt:
Which revision fixes the misplaced modifier?
Arranged in a U-shape, Priya counted the conference chairs before the meeting.

choices.A: Arranged in a U-shape, Priya counted the conference chairs before the meeting.
choices.B: Priya counted the conference chairs arranged in a U-shape before the meeting.
choices.C: Priya, arranged in a U-shape, counted the conference chairs before the meeting.
choices.D: The meeting counted Priya’s chairs arranged before the conference.

questions.correctAnswerText: Priya counted the conference chairs arranged in a U-shape before the meeting.
questions.explanation: This revision places the modifier next to “conference chairs,” the items arranged in a U-shape.

questions.prompt:
Which choice best combines the sentences without changing their meaning?
The supervisor sent a schedule. The schedule listed the training dates.

choices.A: The supervisor sent a schedule that listed the training dates.
choices.B: The schedule sent the supervisor and listed the training dates.
choices.C: Listing the supervisor, the training dates sent a schedule.
choices.D: The supervisor sent, and the training dates listed the schedule.

questions.correctAnswerText: The supervisor sent a schedule that listed the training dates.
questions.explanation: This sentence clearly combines the ideas without changing the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses an apostrophe correctly?

choices.A: The organizer’s checklist was left on the registration table.
choices.B: The organizers checklist was left on the registration table.
choices.C: The organizers’ checklist’s was left on the registration table.
choices.D: The organizer checklists’ was left on the registration table.

questions.correctAnswerText: The organizer’s checklist was left on the registration table.
questions.explanation: The apostrophe correctly shows that the checklist belongs to one organizer.

questions.prompt:
Read both passages. Write an evidence-based essay of 300–500 words explaining which position is better supported. Use evidence from both passages. Maintain a formal tone and organize your response clearly.
Passage A:
Limit Noncompete Agreements for Hourly Workers
States should limit noncompete agreements for hourly workers. A noncompete agreement can prevent a worker from taking a similar job with another employer for a set period of time or within a certain area. These agreements may make sense for executives with access to trade secrets, but they can be unfair when applied to hourly workers who have little control over company strategy.
Hourly workers often need flexibility to improve their income. A cook, delivery dispatcher, salon assistant, or repair clerk may find a better schedule, shorter commute, or higher wage at a competing business. If a noncompete blocks that move, the worker may be stuck in a job that no longer meets basic needs. This limits bargaining power and may keep wages lower.
Businesses already have other ways to protect sensitive information. They can use confidentiality agreements, password controls, customer-data policies, and training on information security. Preventing a worker from accepting a better job is a much broader restriction than protecting confidential records.
A state rule could still allow narrow agreements for workers who truly handle sensitive business information. But broad noncompetes for ordinary hourly jobs go too far. Workers should not have to choose between following an overly broad contract and improving their livelihood.
Passage B:
Allow Businesses to Use Reasonable Noncompete Agreements
States should be careful about limiting noncompete agreements too broadly. Businesses invest time and money in training workers, building customer relationships, and developing methods that help them compete. If employees can immediately take specialized knowledge to a direct competitor, some businesses may become less willing to train workers or share important information.
Noncompete agreements do not have to be unfair. A reasonable agreement can be limited in time, location, and job type. For example, a short restriction for an employee who has direct access to client lists or pricing strategies may protect a business without preventing the person from working in an unrelated role. Instead of banning or sharply limiting noncompetes for a large category of workers, states should require agreements to be specific and reasonable.
Businesses also differ. A small company may be more vulnerable than a large corporation when a trained worker leaves with customer knowledge. If the law removes too much protection, small employers may lose clients they spent years developing.
The better solution is review and enforcement of fair limits. Courts or labor agencies can reject overly broad agreements while allowing reasonable ones that protect legitimate business interests.
Writing Task:
Which passage presents the better-supported argument about whether states should limit noncompete agreements for hourly workers? Write an essay explaining your position. Use evidence from both passages to support your analysis.
Rubric Guidance:
• Analyze which argument is better supported.
• Use relevant evidence from both passages.
• Explain why the evidence supports your claim.
• Address weaknesses or limitations in the less-supported argument.
• Organize ideas logically.
• Use clear sentence structure, grammar, and formal language.
